title: Shell gold gilding
subtitle: Ground gold painted like watercolour and burnished bright
excerpt: Shell gold is finely ground gold powder bound in gum arabic, applied with a small brush in thin layers and burnished to a reflective metallic finish. It is the simplest gilding route for fine calligraphic detail, hairlines, and small decorative elements. Loose-leaf gold on raised gesso produces a higher shine, but shell gold is far more forgiving and requires no specialist gesso-mixing skills.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on illumination and gilding: public domain, Internet Archive. The shell-gold technique is documented by Johnston as the standard approach for small gilded lettering and detail work. Cennino Cennini, Il Libro dell'Arte (c.1400), translated by Daniel V. Thompson as The Craftsman's Handbook (1933): the Italian Renaissance handbook of pigment and gilding techniques; the 1933 translation is public domain in the UK.

glossaryTerms.definition: Finely ground gold powder bound in gum arabic, historically stored in a shell (typically a mussel or clam shell). Applied with a fine brush like a metallic watercolour and burnished to a reflective finish once dry.

A sticky resin dissolved in water and painted onto the surface as a mordant. When breathed upon just before laying gold leaf or shell gold, the moisture reactivates the tack and holds the gold to the surface.
Polishing a dry gilded surface with a hard smooth tool such as an agate burnisher. The friction compresses the gold particles or leaf to produce a mirror-bright, reflective finish.

A chalk-and-lead-white mixture mixed with glair (egg white) and painted onto the writing surface in multiple thin coats to build a raised cushion for loose gold leaf. Provides the platform that gives raised gilding its characteristic relief and exceptional shine.

content.text: Shell gold has been used in illuminated manuscripts since at least the medieval period. Johnston documented it in his chapter on illumination as the right choice for fine calligraphic detail: hairlines drawn in gold with a pointed nib, small letterforms where the precision of a brush matters more than the mirror brilliance achievable with loose-leaf gold on
raised gesso
. The result is subtler than raised gilding but considerably easier to learn. It rewards patience with the drying stages more than any other aspect of the technique.

items.name: Fine brush, size 0 or 000
items.substitutions: A clean pointed watercolour brush in round size 0; sable is preferred

items.name: Watercolour paper, hot-press
items.substitutions: Sized vellum paper if gilding on a traditional surface

items.name: Tracing paper or glassine
items.substitutions: Crystal parchment; use as a slip sheet when burnishing to prevent the burnisher scratching the surrounding area

items.name: Gum ammoniac solution (optional)
items.substitutions: Only needed if a raised surface is wanted rather than flat shell gold

items.name: Pointed nib, Gillott 303 (optional)
items.substitutions: For writing gold hairlines and fine lettering in shell gold directly with a dip pen

attrs.body: Shell gold is not a hazardous material in normal studio use. Work in still air: loose gold (even in its bound form in a pan) lifts on a breath. Gum ammoniac solution has a mild ammonia smell; ensure the room is ventilated when mixing the resin crystals in water.

content.text: The move
Reactivate the
shell gold

. If using a dry pan, add one small drop of water and allow it to dissolve the gum arabic for a few minutes. The gold should reach a consistency similar to thin watercolour paint: fluid enough to flow from the brush without pooling. If it is too thick, add another drop of water. If it looks milky, it has been over-diluted; let some water evaporate before continuing.
Work in still air. Any air movement will cause dried gold fragments to lift from the palette and settle on the wrong part of the paper. Close windows and turn off fans before opening the gold pan.

Apply the first coat of shell gold with the fine brush in the thinnest possible layer. The gold will look patchy and dull at this stage: that is correct. The rule for shell gold is two thin coats rather than one thick one. A thick single application traps moisture underneath, making it almost impossible to burnish to a shine. Allow the first coat to dry completely: at least 15 minutes in a dry room, longer in humid conditions, before the second application.

Apply the second coat directly over the first. The coverage will be more even. Allow this coat to dry to a complete matte finish. Test dryness by pressing the paper on its reverse side at the gilded area: if the spot feels even slightly cool, the gold is still holding moisture and must not be burnished yet.

Place a slip sheet of glassine or tracing paper over the dried gold. The slip sheet protects the surrounding paper from accidental scratches and allows the burnisher to move smoothly without snagging the surface. Hold the slip sheet flat with one finger while
burnishing

with the agate burnisher in firm, circular strokes. Apply steady pressure. Work from the centre of the gilded area outward. After ten to fifteen passes, lift the slip sheet and check: the gold should be visibly brighter. If the surface looks frosted or the gold is tearing, stop: the gold is not fully dry. Return in an hour and try again.

For the highest possible shine, burnish a second time without the slip sheet, pressing the agate directly against the gold surface. This direct burnishing compresses the gold particles more tightly. Use gentle pressure on the first direct pass to avoid tearing the surface, then increase pressure as confidence builds.

To use shell gold as a dip-pen ink for fine lettering: load the Gillott 303 nib with the thinned shell gold using a fine brush. The nib should feel slightly more resistant than with iron-gall ink. Write letters at normal speed. Hairlines will be thin and gold; push strokes will be wide and gold. Allow to dry fully before burnishing, which will be trickier on fine letterforms; use the pointed tip of the agate burnisher carefully.
When a raised surface is wanted: apply
gum ammoniac size

first, allow to dry to a tacky-but-dry finish (usually 30-40 minutes), breathe gently on it to reactivate the tack, and then apply shell gold over the tacky surface. The gold adheres to the raised mordant layer, producing a slight relief.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Shell gold is forgiving in most respects. The one failure that is hard to recover from is burnishing before the gold is completely dry.

items.cause: The gold was not fully dry. Even a small amount of residual moisture in the gum arabic layer causes the gold to pull apart under the burnisher pressure.
items.fix: Stop immediately. Allow the gold to dry for another hour, then try again with the slip sheet using gentle circular strokes rather than direct pressure.

items.symptom: The gold remains dull and matte after burnishing
items.cause: The application may have been too thick, trapping moisture underneath. The paper surface may be too rough. Or the gold may have been burnished too hard, compacting it without polishing.
items.fix: For too-thick application: nothing can be done to that layer; apply a third very thin coat over the burnished surface and burnish again once dry. For a rough surface: switch to hot-press paper, which has a smoother surface than cold-press.

items.symptom: Gold drifts onto the surrounding paper
items.cause: Air movement in the room is lifting dried particles from the palette, or the brush was overloaded.
items.fix: Work in still air. Load the brush from the edge of the pan rather than the centre; a smaller load is easier to place accurately.

items.symptom: The gold looks patchy after two coats
items.cause: The second coat was applied before the first was completely dry, causing the wet gold to lift sections of the first layer.
items.fix: Allow at least 15 minutes between coats. Apply a third coat once the second is fully dry.

content.text: Where it leads

Shell gold is the right starting point for anyone interested in manuscript illumination. Once the drying-and-burnishing sequence is reliable, the next step is raised gesso gilding. This uses a chalk and white-lead ground built up in thin layers, sanded smooth, then gilded with loose-leaf gold. The result is the higher relief and mirror shine of a fully illuminated initial.
